_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Assumption Parish, Louisiana. Record survival, courthouse history, and known losses for this Bayou Lafourche corridor parish with records stretching to the French colonial period. Assumption is one of Acadiana's oldest parishes, with Acadian settlement along Bayou Lafourche dating to the 1760s and sugar plantation records from the antebellum era. Louisiana uses Napoleonic Code tradition -- successions, conveyances, and notarial archives rather than common-law probate.

current_courthouse.location: Napoleonville, LA -- 4809 Highway 1. Modern courthouse replacing the antebellum structure.

previous_courthouses.location: Napoleonville -- early frame courthouse on Bayou Lafourche
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced by a substantial brick courthouse in the 1850s as the sugar economy brought prosperity to the parish.

previous_courthouses.location: Napoleonville -- antebellum brick courthouse on Bayou Lafourche, one of the finest small-town courthouses in Louisiana
previous_courthouses.fate: The antebellum courthouse survived the Civil War, multiple hurricanes, and over a century of use. It was replaced by a modern building in 1975 but the old structure still stands as a historic landmark. Records were transferred to the new building.

record_losses.cause: Civil War -- Federal occupation of the Bayou Lafourche corridor
records_destroyed: some scattered loose papers and militia records
record_losses.description: Federal forces occupied the Bayou Lafourche corridor in 1862 following the Battle of Labadieville (October 1862). The courthouse at Napoleonville was not systematically targeted, and bound record books survived. Some loose papers and Confederate-era militia records were lost or taken. The primary disruption was administrative -- the parish government was interrupted during Federal occupation, creating gaps in contemporary record-keeping rather than destroying earlier records.
record_losses.narrative_impact: The Federal occupation of Bayou Lafourche disrupted record-keeping for a period, but Assumption Parish's earlier records -- including the invaluable colonial-era and antebellum succession inventories that document enslaved people by name -- survived intact.
record_losses.source: Winters, John D. The Civil War in Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1963.

record_losses.cause: Hurricanes -- multiple storms caused water damage to records over the decades
records_destroyed: some loose papers sustained water damage in various storms
records_survived: bound record books generally survived due to their elevation and binding
record_losses.description: Assumption Parish, located in the low-lying Bayou Lafourche corridor, has experienced multiple hurricanes. Individual storm events caused minor water damage to loose papers, but the parish's bound record books -- conveyances, successions, and marriage registers -- survived in generally good condition. The construction of the modern courthouse in 1975 improved record storage conditions.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Hurricane damage in Assumption Parish has been incremental rather than catastrophic. The parish's records are remarkably intact for a low-lying Louisiana parish, preserving a documentary record that stretches from the colonial period through the sugar plantation era.
record_losses.source: Riffel, Judy. Assumption Parish: The Way It Was. Gonzales, LA: Assumption Parish Library, 1993.

deeds.completeness: Good -- continuous conveyance records from early statehood. Some colonial-era land grants are recorded in the early conveyance books as claims were re-registered under American law.
deeds.gaps: Pre-1807 land records are fragmentary and must be supplemented with colonial archives. Some early conveyances are in French.
deeds.repository: Assumption Parish Clerk of Court, Napoleonville
deeds.online_access: Limited -- some indexes through Louisiana Secretary of State

wills_estates.completeness: Good -- succession records are continuous and particularly rich for the sugar plantation era. Antebellum succession inventories are extraordinarily detailed, listing enslaved people by name, age, and assessed value along with land, livestock, and equipment.
wills_estates.gaps: Colonial-era successions are at the Louisiana State Archives. Some early records are in French and require translation.
wills_estates.repository: Assumption Parish Clerk of Court, Napoleonville; Louisiana State Archives for colonial records

marriages.completeness: Good -- civil marriage records from early statehood. Catholic sacramental registers from Assumption Church in Plattenville and other parishes are significantly earlier (1790s).
marriages.gaps: Colonial-era marriages documented only in Catholic church records. Civil records occasionally in French through the 1840s.
marriages.repository: Assumption Parish Clerk of Court; Diocese of Houma-Thibodaux archives
marriages.online_access: Some marriage indexes at Louisiana Secretary of State; Catholic records partly abstracted in Hebert

court_minutes.completeness: Good -- Police Jury minutes and court records continuous from early statehood
court_minutes.gaps: Some gaps during the Federal occupation (1862-1863). Early records are in French.
court_minutes.repository: Assumption Parish Clerk of Court, Napoleonville

vital_records.completeness: Fair -- statewide registration began in 1898 but compliance was slow in rural Bayou Lafourche communities. Church baptismal and burial records are more reliable for the early registration period.
vital_records.repository: Louisiana Office of Vital Records, New Orleans

substitute_sources.source_type: Catholic sacramental records -- Assumption Church and others
substitute_sources.description: Catholic parish records along Bayou Lafourche predate civil records by decades. Assumption Church at Plattenville, St. Elizabeth at Paincourtville, and other churches maintained baptism, marriage, and burial registers from the late 1700s. These records are in French and Latin.
substitute_sources.repository: Individual parish churches; Diocese of Houma-Thibodaux archives; some abstracted in Hebert
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Indispensable for Acadian and Creole families. Baptismal records name both parents and godparents (parrains/marraines). Marriage records include parents' names and often birthplaces. Burial records sometimes note cause of death and age.
substitute_sources.source: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.

substitute_sources.source_type: Sugar plantation records and succession inventories
substitute_sources.description: Antebellum succession inventories for Assumption Parish planters are among the most detailed records in Louisiana genealogy. They list every enslaved person by name, age, and sometimes family grouping, along with detailed inventories of land, equipment, and livestock. These records are the primary source for tracing enslaved families in the parish.
substitute_sources.repository: Assumption Parish Clerk of Court; some plantation records at LSU Special Collections
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Succession inventories are the single most important source for enslaved family research in Assumption Parish. They provide names, ages, family groupings, and assessed values that can be cross-referenced with slave schedules and post-emancipation records.
substitute_sources.source: Riffel, Judy. Assumption Parish: The Way It Was. Gonzales, LA: Assumption Parish Library, 1993.

substitute_sources.description: Louisiana's notarial system, inherited from French and Spanish colonial law, required that significant transactions (property sales, marriage contracts, apprenticeships, emancipations) be recorded before a notary public. Notarial archives survive for Assumption Parish from the early 1800s and contain documents not found in the standard courthouse records.
substitute_sources.repository: Assumption Parish Clerk of Court; Louisiana State Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Notarial records include marriage contracts (contrats de mariage) that detail property each spouse brought to the marriage -- an invaluable genealogical source under Louisiana's community property system. They also include emancipation acts, apprenticeship agreements, and private property transactions.
substitute_sources.source: Conrad, Glenn R. The French Experience in Louisiana. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1995.

narrative_hooks: Assumption Parish's succession inventories are a genealogist's treasure -- they list every enslaved person by name alongside the furniture, livestock, and sugar equipment, making them the primary source for tracing {ancestor_name}'s family if they were enslaved on a Bayou Lafourche plantation.

The Catholic church at Plattenville recorded {ancestor_name}'s baptism in French, naming the parrains and marraines whose selection reveals the kinship alliances that defined Acadian community life along Bayou Lafourche.

Louisiana's notarial system means {ancestor_name}'s marriage contract -- the contrat de mariage -- may survive at the courthouse, detailing what each spouse brought to the marriage in a way that Anglo-American records never do.

If {ancestor_name} lived along Bayou Lafourche before 1807, their records are in French and Spanish colonial archives -- a different legal system, a different language, and a different understanding of property and family than the American records that followed.
source_references: Hebert, Donald J. Southwest Louisiana Records. 39 vols. Eunice, LA: self-published, 1974-2000.
Riffel, Judy. Assumption Parish: The Way It Was. Gonzales, LA: Assumption Parish Library, 1993.
Winters, John D. The Civil War in Louisiana. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1963.
Conrad, Glenn R. The French Experience in Louisiana. Lafayette: Center for Louisiana Studies, 1995.
Brasseaux, Carl A. The Founding of New Acadia: The Beginnings of Acadian Life in Louisiana, 1765-1803. Baton Rouge: LSU Press, 1987.
